items.q: What did Brown v Board of Education decide in 1954?
items.correct: The Supreme Court ruled that 'separate but equal' schools were inherently unequal, overturning Plessy v Ferguson and beginning legal dismantling of school segregation.
items.misconception: Brown v Board of Education immediately desegregated all schools in America.
items.random_wrong: Brown v Board ended slavery in the southern states.
items.why: Implementation was patchy and resisted (e.g. Little Rock 1957). The ruling was a legal foundation, not instant change — students assume court decisions equal social reality.

items.q: How did Malcolm X's approach differ from MLK's?
items.correct: MLK preached nonviolent integration; Malcolm X (until 1964) advocated black self-defence, separatism, and rejected integration as a goal — though both shifted positions over time.
items.misconception: Malcolm X advocated violent terrorism against white Americans.
items.random_wrong: Malcolm X and MLK were close allies who jointly led the March on Washington.
items.why: Malcolm X advocated self-defence, not aggressive terrorism. Students contrast them too sharply; both critiqued American racism, with Malcolm X moderating after his 1964 Hajj.

items.q: How was the US civil rights movement achieved?
items.correct: Through decades of mass organising — NAACP legal work, grassroots campaigns, sit-ins, freedom rides — alongside leaders like King and Malcolm X.
items.misconception: Martin Luther King's 'I Have a Dream' speech ended segregation.
items.random_wrong: Lincoln ended segregation by signing the 14th Amendment.
items.why: Students reduce the movement to one leader and one speech. The 1964 Civil Rights Act resulted from sustained pressure by thousands of activists, not a single moment of inspiration.

items.q: Which 1954 Supreme Court ruling overturned 'separate but equal' in schools?
items.why: Correct answer: Brown v Board. “Plessy v Ferguson” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In which year did the Voting Rights Act become law?
items.why: Correct answer: 1965. “1957” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did the Watergate break-in occur?
items.why: The real figure is 1972. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
